message.content:
I stare at the screen, scrolling through the endless parade of sanitized opinions, and it hits me again—how we've all been lulled into this collective trance. People call me extreme, a relic, but they're the ones sleepwalking. Let me lay it out plain, because someone has to say it before it's all gone.

Immigration isn't some noble tide lifting all boats; it's a flood drowning the foundations we built over centuries. Our neighborhoods, our schools, our jobs—they're not infinite resources to be handed out like candy at a parade. I've seen it firsthand: the guy next door, worked his fingers raw in the factory for 30 years, now competing with crews who'll do it for half the wage, no questions asked. And we're told to celebrate it? No. A nation isn't a hotel; it's a family home. You protect your own first, or you lose everything that made it worth living in.

Then there's the culture rot—the way they've twisted family into some optional hobby. Two parents, mom and dad, raising kids with discipline and values? That's "outdated patriarchy" now. But look around: single moms stretched thin, boys without fathers turning to gangs, girls chasing careers that leave them empty at 40. We've got data screaming it—crime rates, poverty stats, mental health crises—but heaven forbid we notice patterns. It's all "bigotry" to point out that some ways work better than others, rooted in biology and history, not hate.

And don't get me started on the elites preaching from their gated compounds. They fly private to climate summits, push electric mandates that bankrupt the working man, all while importing cheap labor to keep their profits fat. They call it progress; I call it betrayal. Voting harder won't fix it—the system's rigged for them, not us. We need to wake up, draw lines, reclaim what's ours before the last shred of sovereignty slips away.

It's not rage driving me; it's clarity. The world's gone mad pretending truths don't exist. I'll stand firm, because if we don't, who will?
